en.extract:
The Emei Shan liocichla (Liocichla omeiensis), also known as the Omei Shan or grey-faced liocichla, is a passerine bird in the family Leiothrichidae.  It is closely related to the Bugun liocichla, a species only described in 2006, and which it closely resembles. 


Habitat
The species is endemic to forests in mountain ranges in Southern Sichuan, China. It is an altitudinal migrant, spending the summer months above 1000 m and moving below 600m in the winter. It is often found in thick bamboo and deep scrub forests.


Description
The Emei Shan liocichla is an olive-grey coloured bird with red wing patches. The plumage on the face is grey with a slight red ring on each side of the face. 


Diet
The species feeds in the undergrowth of semi-tropical rainforest. It is an herbivore.


Threats
The Emei Shan liocichla is considered vulnerable by the IUCN. It is threatened by habitat loss through logging and conversion to agriculture. Some populations are protected inside reserves, such as the Emei Shan Protected Scenic Site.


Vocalization
Individual males have very distinct songs, and can be identified by their vocalizations. They whistle loudly and in complex patterns during breeding seasons to maintain their territories. Females do not vocalize.


Breeding
Its nesting success has decreased as a result of habitat loss. After disturbances, Emei Shan liocichlas tend to place their nests higher (in taller plants or on higher parts of the plants), or in thornier trees, presumably to protect against further disturbances.


References

Birdlife International Bugun Liocichla: a sensational discovery in north-east IndiaDownloaded from Archived 2007-07-10 at the Wayback Machine on 12/9/2006
BirdLife International (2006) Species factsheet: Liocichla omeiensis. Downloaded from https://web.archive.org/web/20240303201308/https://www.birdlife.org/ on 12/9/2006
Collar, N. J. & Robson C. 2007. Family Timaliidae (Babblers)  pp. 70 – 291 in; del Hoyo, J., Elliott, A. & Christie, D.A. eds. Handbook of the Birds of the World, Vol. 12. Picathartes to Tits and Chickadees. Lynx Edicions, Barcelona.


External links
Image at ADW

The '''Emei Shan liocichla''' ('''''Liocichla omeiensis'''''), also known as the '''Omei Shan''' or '''grey-faced liocichla''', is a [[passerine]] bird in the [[Family (taxonomy)|family]] [[Leiothrichidae]].  It is closely related to the [[Bugun liocichla]], a species only described in 2006, and which it closely resembles. 

== Habitat ==
The species is [[endemic (ecology)|endemic]] to forests<ref name=":0">{{Cite journal |last=Kong |first=Chiping |last2=Wu |first2=Yongheng |last3=Lou |first3=Shangling |last4=Chen |first4=Benping |last5=Dowell |first5=Simon D. |last6=Fu |first6=Yiqiang |date=2020 |title=Individual Identification Based on the Songs of the Emei Shan Liocichla (Liocichla omeiensis) |journal=Pakistan Journal of Zoology |volume=52 |issue=2 |doi=10.17582/journal.pjz/20170510120508 |issn=0030-9923|doi-access=free }}</ref> in mountain ranges in Southern [[Sichuan]], [[China]]. It is an altitudinal [[bird migration|migrant]], spending the summer months above 1000 m and moving below 600m in the winter.<ref name="iucn status 12 November 2021" /> It is often found in thick [[bamboo]] and deep [[Shrubland|scrub forests]].<ref name=":0" />

== Description ==
The Emei Shan liocichla is an olive-grey coloured bird with red wing patches. The [[plumage]] on the face is grey with a slight red ring on each side of the face.<ref name="iucn status 12 November 2021" /> 

== Diet ==
The species feeds in the [[Understory|undergrowth]] of [[Subtropics|semi-tropical]] [[rainforest]].<ref name="iucn status 12 November 2021" /> It is an herbivore.<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Huang |first=R |last2=Li |first2=L |last3=Song |first3=B |last4=Lyu |first4=Y |last5=Wu |first5=B |date=2020 |title=Appearance and Digestive System Comparison of Lonchura Striata and Copsychus Saularis: Searching for the Effect of Staple Feeding Ingredients on Avian Morphology |journal=Brazilian Journal of Poultry Science |volume=22 |issue=4 |doi=10.1590/1806-9061-2020-1308 |issn=1806-9061|doi-access=free }}</ref>

== Threats ==
The Emei Shan liocichla is considered vulnerable by the [[IUCN]]. It is threatened by [[habitat loss]] through [[logging]] and conversion to [[agriculture]]. Some populations are protected inside [[Nature reserve|reserves]], such as the [[Emei Shan Protected Scenic Site]].<ref name="iucn status 12 November 2021" />

== Vocalization ==
Individual males have very distinct songs, and can be identified by their vocalizations.<ref name=":0" /> They whistle loudly and in complex patterns during breeding seasons to maintain their territories.<ref name=":0" /> Females do not vocalize.<ref name=":0" />

== Breeding ==
Its nesting success has decreased as a result of habitat loss.<ref name=":1">{{Cite journal |last=Fu |first=Yiqiang |last2=Xu |first2=Yuanyuan |last3=Wang |first3=Shufang |last4=Dowell |first4=Simon D. |last5=Zhang |first5=Zhengwang |date=2022-04-12 |title=Threatened songbird ''Liocichla omeiensis'' impacted by climate‐induced outbreak of the moth ''Pantana phyllostachysae'': An example of the impact of climate change through multi‐species interactions |journal=Conservation Science and Practice |volume=4 |issue=6 |doi=10.1111/csp2.12691 |issn=2578-4854|doi-access=free }}</ref> After disturbances, Emei Shan liocichlas tend to place their nests higher (in taller plants or on higher parts of the plants), or in thornier trees, presumably to protect against further disturbances.<ref name=":1" />
